title: From Homeschooling to Executive Coaching: Building Leaders at Home and Work
guestBio: Amanda Jaggard is an Executive Coach at Novus Global and co-founder of the Meta Performance Institute. After earning her Master's in Leadership and Ministry and homeschooling her sons for nearly a decade, she transitioned to executive coaching in 2014. As a single mother, she has successfully raised leaders while building a thriving coaching practice that helps others develop both their coaching skills and business acumen.
excerpt: Executive coach Amanda Jaggard shares how she transformed from homeschooling mother to successful business leader while raising her sons on commitments instead of rules. Her powerful approach to creating culture at home offers game-changing lessons for leadership in every area of life.
metaDescription: Executive coach Amanda Jaggard reveals how parenting with commitments instead of rules builds leaders at home and transforms your approach to business culture.
keyTakeaways: Commitments create stronger foundations than rules because they require personal ownership and buy-in from everyone involved.
Creating intentional culture at home through language, agreements, and respect directly translates to better leadership skills in business.
The transition from homeschooling to entrepreneurship requires recognizing that your parenting experience has developed valuable business skills.
Building a coaching practice successfully requires both mastering the art of coaching and developing strong business fundamentals.
Preparing for empty nest season while your children are still home allows for a smoother transition and stronger family relationships.
Single mothers can raise confident leaders by modeling resilience and teaching children to take ownership of their choices.
Executive coaching principles applied to parenting create households where everyone feels respected and empowered to grow.

pullQuotes.text: We raised our boys on commitments instead of rules, and it changed everything about how our family operated.

timestamps.label: Amanda's journey from homeschooling to executive coaching

content:
What if everything you think you know about creating structure in your home and business is backwards? What if instead of imposing rules from the top down, you could build something far more powerful from the ground up? That's exactly what today's guest Amanda Jaggard discovered when she transformed her approach to both parenting and leadership, and the results will challenge everything you think you know about raising leaders.

Amanda's story is one that will resonate deeply with any woman who has felt the tension between nurturing her family and building her career. After earning her Master's in Leadership and Ministry, she spent nearly a decade as a homeschooling mother, pouring herself into raising her two sons. But in 2014, she made a pivotal decision to step fully into executive coaching, and by 2017, she had joined Novus Global, where she helped build their unique franchise model and co-created the Meta Performance Institute.

What makes Amanda's perspective so valuable isn't just her professional success, but how she's seamlessly integrated the principles of executive coaching into her role as a single mother. She's raised one son who now attends UCLA and has another approaching high school graduation, and her approach offers profound insights for anyone looking to build stronger culture both at home and in business.

The Power of Commitments Over Rules

One of the most transformative concepts Amanda shares is the radical difference between operating from rules versus commitments. In traditional households and many businesses, we default to rules because they feel safer and more controlled. Rules are imposed from authority figures and followed out of obligation or fear of consequences. But Amanda discovered something remarkable when she shifted her family dynamic to operate from commitments instead.

Commitments require something completely different from everyone involved. They demand conversation, buy-in, and personal ownership. When her family moved from you must do this because I said so to what are we all committing to and why does this matter to us, everything changed. Her sons began taking genuine ownership of their choices rather than simply complying or rebelling against external expectations.

This shift creates leaders rather than followers. When you raise children on commitments, you're teaching them to think critically about their choices, to understand the impact of their decisions on others, and to operate from internal motivation rather than external pressure. These are exactly the qualities we desperately need in business leaders, and Amanda proves that we can cultivate them from the earliest ages.

Creating Intentional Culture at Home

Amanda's approach to building family culture offers a masterclass in leadership that directly translates to business success. She understood that culture doesn't happen by accident, whether you're leading a household or a corporation. It requires intentional language, clear agreements, and a foundation built on mutual respect and honor.

In her home, this meant having real conversations about what kind of family they wanted to be, what values would guide their decisions, and how they would handle conflicts when they arose. It meant treating her sons as valuable contributors to the family culture rather than recipients of rules handed down from above. This approach required more initial investment in conversation and relationship-building, but it created a foundation that could weather the storms of adolescence and single parenthood.

The brilliance of this approach becomes clear when you consider how it prepares both parents and children for success in every other area of life. Children who grow up participating in the creation of family culture understand how to contribute meaningfully to workplace culture. They know how to have difficult conversations, how to negotiate agreements that work for everyone involved, and how to take responsibility for their part in making systems function well.

Building a Business While Raising Leaders

Amanda's journey from homeschooling mother to successful executive coach and co-founder of the Meta Performance Institute demonstrates something crucial that many women need to hear: your experience as a mother has prepared you for business success in ways you might not even recognize yet. The skills required to successfully homeschool children, manage a household, and create thriving family culture are directly transferable to building and leading businesses.

The Meta Performance Institute, which Amanda co-founded, addresses a critical gap in the coaching industry. While many programs focus solely on developing coaching skills, Amanda recognized that coaches also need strong business fundamentals to build sustainable practices. This insight came directly from her experience as a mother and educator, understanding that you need both the heart skills and the practical skills to create lasting impact.

Her success in building this institute while raising her sons as a single mother proves that you don't have to choose between being fully present for your children and building something significant professionally. Instead, the key is recognizing how these roles can strengthen and inform each other when approached with intention and strategic thinking.

Preparing for What's Next

One of the most honest parts of Amanda's story involves her preparation for the empty nest season. Rather than waiting until her sons left home to figure out what comes next, she's been intentionally building her career and identity alongside her role as their mother. This preparation isn't about rushing toward the next phase of life, but about ensuring that when the time comes for her sons to launch into their own lives, she'll be ready to launch into hers as well.

This approach benefits everyone in the family. Her sons get to see their mother as a whole person with her own dreams and ambitions, not just someone whose entire identity revolves around their needs. They learn that healthy adults continue growing and pursuing meaningful work throughout their lives. And Amanda gets to model what it looks like to be a woman who embraces all aspects of her identity without apology.

Amanda Jaggard's story offers a powerful reminder that the invisible work of motherhood is indeed leadership, and the skills you're developing in that role are preparing you for impact far beyond your home. Whether you're homeschooling, working full-time, or somewhere in between, you have the capacity to create culture, build commitments that matter, and raise the next generation of leaders while building something meaningful for yourself.

quiz.title: What's Your Leadership Building Style?
quiz.description: Discover how you naturally create structure and empower others, whether at home, work, or in your community. This quiz reveals your unique approach to building leaders around you.

questions.question: When you need to establish new expectations in your family or team, what's your go-to approach?

options.text: I create clear guidelines and systems that everyone can follow consistently
I bring everyone together to discuss what we all think would work best
I start with something flexible and adjust based on how it's working for different people
I focus on helping each person understand how this connects to their personal growth

questions.question: When someone you're leading makes a mistake, your first instinct is to:

options.text: Review what systems or processes might need to be strengthened
Have a conversation about what happened and what we can learn together
Figure out what approach might work better for this specific person
Help them reflect on what this experience can teach them about themselves

questions.question: You feel most successful as a leader when:

options.text: The people around you have reliable structures that help them thrive
Everyone feels like they have a voice and ownership in our shared goals
You can meet each person's unique needs while moving toward the same vision
The people you lead are growing into their own leadership capabilities

questions.question: When planning for the future, whether family goals or business objectives, you typically:

options.text: Create a solid framework that can guide decisions and actions over time
Make sure everyone is aligned on the vision before moving forward
Keep the big picture in mind while staying open to changing circumstances
Focus on how this journey will develop everyone involved

questions.question: Your friends and family would say your greatest strength in relationships is:

options.text: Being someone others can count on to create stability and consistency
Making sure everyone feels included and heard in important decisions
Understanding what different people need and adapting your support accordingly
Helping others see their own potential and encouraging their growth

questions.question: When you think about your legacy as a leader, you most want to be remembered for:

options.text: Building something lasting that continues to benefit others long after you're gone
Creating environments where everyone felt valued and had a voice
Being able to bring out the best in people by meeting them where they were
Developing other leaders who went on to make their own positive impact

results.title: The Foundation Builder
results.description: You believe in creating strong systems and structures that everyone can rely on. You naturally think about long-term impact and love establishing frameworks that help others thrive. Your strength lies in building something solid that lasts, whether that's family traditions, team processes, or organizational culture. This episode will show you how to transform your natural structure-building gifts by shifting from rules to commitments, creating even deeper buy-in from those you lead.

results.title: The Collaborative Creator
results.description: You thrive on bringing people together and building consensus before moving forward. You instinctively know that the best solutions come from involving everyone in the conversation. Your superpower is making others feel heard and valued in the decision-making process. Amanda's insights about creating commitments through conversation and buy-in will resonate deeply with your collaborative nature and give you practical tools to strengthen your approach both at home and work.

results.title: The Adaptive Navigator
results.description: You excel at reading the room and adjusting your approach based on what each situation or person needs. You're comfortable with flexibility and believe that leadership looks different in different contexts. Your strength is in your ability to pivot and meet people where they are. This episode will help you see how your adaptability is actually a form of intentional culture-building, and give you frameworks to channel your natural flexibility into even more powerful leadership.

results.title: The Empowerment Catalyst
results.description: You're naturally focused on developing others and helping them step into their own power. You believe deeply that everyone has leadership potential waiting to be unlocked. Your gift is seeing people's capabilities before they see them themselves and creating space for growth. Amanda's journey from homeschooling to executive coaching perfectly mirrors your development-focused approach, and this episode will inspire new ways to nurture the leaders around you.

transcript: [music and singing] Hi everyone and welcome back to the manager, the podcast where we turn the invisible work of women into visible leadership power. I'm your host Amy Rickabus, entrepreneur, author of The Manager, and mama of six. Today's guest is someone whose story will resonate with so many of us who've balanced raising a family while building a career. Amanda Jagard is an executive coach with Novvice Global and the co-founder of the Meta Performance Institute, where she trains coaches not only in coaching itself, but also in the business skills it takes to thrive. Amanda has been a coach since 2014, a single mom since 2018. And now, as she prepares to become an empty neester, she's building her career with the same passion she used to build a home culture of respect, honor, and commitment with her two sons. From her master's degree in leadership and ministry to shaping the next generation of coaches to raising men who are about to launch into the world, Amanda's story is all about resilience, leadership, and creating culture with intention. Amanda, it's awesome to have you on the show. I'm so glad you're here with me today. This is going to be so much fun. >> Thank you for having me, Amy. I'm really excited about the conversation. >> I know. Me, too. Okay, so let's get into it. So, you started coaching in 2014, but before that, you spent years homeschooling your boys. Can you share what the transition looked like from homeschool education to building a professional career? >> Yes. So, I did my training while I was still homeschooling. That was that was possible. That went um that went great cuz we were both, you know, doing things at the same time. You do your school, I'll do my school. And then I thought that I could uh homeschool and do the kids thing at at home also. And um with my older one, that went fine. And he's always done a hybrid type education like all the way through it was really designed towards what was going to work for him. But the younger one, he just needs to bounce or needed to bounce at the time. And so, yes, there was a moment I was on a coaching call um with a senior vice president of a company and I heard Elijah running around on the roof. And so that was like [laughter] the with this isn't gonna yeah it's not it's not going to work. Um so then found a place for for him to go. Um but still I think the nature of my of of coaching I really did continue to just to bring them along with me. Like I remember I was thinking back and I remember we were working with a a school system and I was going to do an on-site thing and I was like Xander do you want to come with me? and he would have been in middle school. Um, but he did like he came along and it was appropriate for the setting because it was a school setting. Um, but I think that there was like yes, they were in school but they also join me in a lot of the professional stuff too. I think it was a transition for them because I remember we were in an argument one time. They're like, "You're a stay-at-home mom." I was like, "I no, I work from home. Those are two different things like because I think in their mind it was a transition for them too of I actually had things to do here. So all of the the boundary setting um and that but uh it was a transition for all of us for sure. >> Totally. Yeah. Changing their expectations like mommy's still going to be here but she's going to be on calls and you can't just walk in and ask her for a cookie. >> That's right. and they are old enough like they knew but still um I have I look over I have a sign on the door that says do not disturb and Xander made it I think during COVID and it's like unless there's like a list of all of these things like one includes like a nuclear fallout and you know that's like everybody's like there it's like y'all but still even like my 17-year-old the other day I was on with a client and he like walks in and I was like dude like you know you know and so but I think that they're used to to sometimes in company meetings and they know the people in our company and they hop on and say hello. So, um >> that's sweet. That's sweet. It's so like when you raise them in the business, it's different. But yeah, I know. I was just I was just doing some work from home and I had my 16-year-old walk in, you know, in the background of one of the calls I was on and I'm like, uh, dude, >> dude, >> dude, [laughter] >> we should know this by now. No longer. >> The life of the homeschooling mother. I'm like, h >> that's great. That's great. [laughter] >> So, you've been with Novvice since 2017, right? >> And you helped to co-ound the Meta Performance Institute. What makes your approach to coaching so different from the other coaching models that are out there? >> I love this question. In fact, I was just on a call earlier today with uh a person. she has gotten trained through Berkeley and then with ICF um and we were talking because she was asking the same thing. The coaching that we do like metaperformance coaching it still is very inquiry based like that coaching is asking questions like you don't hire me because of what I know. Um, so it's not the consulting and it's not mentoring. It's not, "Yeah, I have led a, you know, $10 million company. I know what that's like. Here, let me help you along." Right? It it is the getting curious and asking questions and the inquiry. And I would say the type of coaching we do is not purely like neutral. Like there is this yes, the wisdom is in the client and we want to pull that out. But I think what we would say is the vision is the boss. I'm not the VA boss. The client isn't the boss. The vision, whatever it is that they decide they're going after, that is what kind of we all answer to. And everything is in service of that. So there is a bias. The bias is the vision. And I would say the kind of coaching we do too um we uh allow encourage um disruption and that's like so it's not just like oh you're great and oh that's wonderful like usually as people are going um you know what got you here isn't going to get you there and so we want to listen and as quickly as possible in there's so many different ways to ing about disruption but disrupt what either that current way of thinking is um that's might be getting in their way um in order to create something new. So that's I think where it's not as just ne neutral and open. There is um a fun fierce advocacy that is there for the vision and for the person that uh involves disrupting things a little bit. >> I love that cuz like you say things get messy when you're working with people. [laughter] So they totally do and the the driving metaphor that you guys use at the MetaPerformance Institute is that you learn coaching not just in a classroom but by actually coaching and being coached. Could you expand on that? >> Yeah. Um my so my kids now are 20 and 17 and Xander the the 20-year-old he's um at college uh and he's currently going through our institute because he's just been raised around it. So so fun. Um but we were talking and he was like mom I just like I want to sit in a classroom and I want to learn this stuff and you know have the the curriculum and step by step and this is what you learn how. I was like, "Dude, like you know, you didn't learn to drive by doing that 30hour online whatever that you actually really didn't pay attention to. Like that is not what made you a good driver. >> You learned by actually the practice driving and being in the car, being behind the wheel and getting your hours in for that. So with the metaperformance institute um the way that we've said it it's almost like three parts where uh I really believe that co way you learn how to coach by actually doing the work in ourselves first. I think it's not necessarily required but for me it is like I think how I'm able to serve and show up for people comes from the hours I've spent with myself and with the work and the disruption and the advocacy. So, there's a lot of um coaching that you receive and then you get your reps in by actually doing it. And then there's the business development side of things, too, which a lot of coaching programs don't include. So, it's like all three of those things all laid over together. That's what's included in the five-month program. That's so cool. Yeah. Business development is often overlooked with the coaching. You know, you they're teaching someone to coach like, "Okay, here's the program. figure it out yourself. We're not going to coach you and um we're not going to do any business development along with it. So it's like, you know, there's a high failure rate. >> Yeah. Yeah. And I think people get the skills of doing it. Um so they might be uh you know be able to add value to people's lives, but if people if you don't actually have the business development side where there is a economic exchange in for those services, right? and you're actually building a coaching practice and you're raising your rates and you're navigating how all of that works. If you can't do that, then I think people there's a lot that just stay kind of in that either hobbyist space or they're not able it's like a side thing um to their real job. We really want to um like really professionalize the craft like because it's such a noble work to be able to sit across from someone and listen to them in the most intimate of ways and advocate for their dreams and what they want more than anything. Like it's it's just right like it's it's like can we professionalize that almost like medical or lawyer like it's not just a hobby. it like actually is where people are creating six figure coaching practices to provide for their family, do good work in the world. That's really what we want to be able to set people up to do. >> Creating a value around that. >> Yeah. >> Creating value. Yeah. My mom always said it's better to not do the work and not get paid than to do the work and not get paid. So, it's one of those important things in life to learn when you're running your business that it is important that you set contracts and that you have agreements with your clients and that they know how much they're going to pay you and when they're going to pay you and those kinds of things. It's >> and I think some people come into coaching where they maybe have done this type of work and not gotten paid. So either they were doing it as leaders in companies, but it wasn't necessarily part of their job description to where they were getting paid, they just loved it. Or people were in like ministry settings where they were doing it, but maybe not getting paid very much. >> Um or I think for me, I was a volunteer in a lot of ways and I wasn't getting paid. And so there is almost this like um I would do this for free like yeah, I would talk to people all day. And so the the mindset shift I think of what we make up about doing something that we would do for free that we love and then putting a an economic co you know value to that. >> Um is a transition for a lot of people and I think that probably gets in the way of people professionalizing it. Um, so we talk all about like the first intensive we start talking about all of those beliefs that we have around that and shifting some of those. >> Yeah. Shifting beliefs around money and your value and all of those things. I think that's so important. It's definitely something we talk about in the book. But one of the themes that we talk about in the manager is that mothers are the culture makers of the home. And you've created a family culture around words like honor and respect and agreement. How did you exe your executive coaching principles shape your parenting? >> A lot that I think I'm so grateful because I think I got trained and I got trained in 2014. So the boys would have been like nine and six. Um and I think I already always had principles of ownership and coming from a background of education but having those tools the even the visioning of what's the end result that we're going after here um like so was my statement was like I raise men and that is what like there is the vision >> and so that then began to inform if if our vision is the boss and then vision informs the strategy, right? So, that informed the strategy of the the parent and like we just I um sometimes we'll joke like coaches talk weird like we use real word real words like um as it occurs to me and what I make up about that is and commitment and ownership. And the other day, the day my 17-year-old, we were driving and we were talking about something with his his volleyball coach and he was like, "We have the same amount of believability." I was like and he was I was like I just I can we just say that I love that you use that word and he was like I know I talk I talk weird. I was like you love it. And he was like I do. It makes me sound smart. Um but he used it appropriately. Um so they use words like empowerment, right? And um and I think that that's it's in sitting I mean I've sat in a lot of 12step rooms with adults talking about their families of origin. >> Um I've worked through a lot of my stuff with my family of origin and reparenting myself and the things that I needed to learn in my 20s and 30s um that I did tools that I didn't have growing up. And so I think having my kids it was like what if like because we'll coach people in companies are like I wish I would have had this 20 or 30 years ago like like experiment to me of like what if all of these things that I get paid to like coach out of people and then coach into them. What if these humans in when they're they have singledigit of ages, they could actually have these mindsets and these tools. what would that what would that begin to look like? Like who would they become? How would they how would they do junior high? How would they do high school? How would they do college? Um so I'm getting to see that play out in real time these days. >> I just absolutely love it. I just been since we talked, since we had our pre-talk, I have been non-stop thinking about the fact now when I talk to my kids, I'm like, you know, I'm not raising children, right? I'm raising future husbands and fathers. you know, when I'm talking to the boys and they're like, "Oh, I walked into Gabriel's room this morning and I said, "Sweetheart, you're gonna make a wonderful husband one day and you're gonna be such a good dad." And I think it's setting that vision for them. You know, I think that we need to put those ideas in their heads that they're going to be good at those things, that they're going to be, you know, whether they decide to do it or not, that they're going to be good at those things because that's such an important subconscious, >> you know, mom gives you a lot of your subconscious talk. >> Yeah. So it's like whether it's true or not. >> Yes. >> It's nice to have those good that good subconscious talk you know programmed right into their minds. Even I think you were talking like the transition of uh you going professionally and like being home like we had they had gone to school and then co happened so we were all here again but they overhear like me they hear the language that I use in trainings or and talking to clients and and it is the same things the the same conversation that I have with an executive CEO is the same conversation that I have with them like um well what are you committed to? Well, you know, what's what's your vision for that? Or um like the tapes that I want them to be playing of like it's all unfolding perfectly. This is happening for me. How might I be complicit in this situation? Where is the ownership that I can take? Like all every conversation that I'm having with my client is 100% the same conversation that we're having in a car. And I'm not coaching them all the time. Like they they know. Um uh I don't know. Uh, one time was like because we say like wave a magic wand. I like wave a magic wand. What do you want? It's like I don't want to wave effing magic wand. I was like I get it. I get it. Get it. Get it. So like they they let me know when it's like stop. And I know when to just be like just listen and like now is not the time to coach. But um the the conversations it's just it's a human conversation and it's a human conversation of us moving into who we were created and empowered to be as human beings no matter what age we are. >> Yeah. I love it. I love when you talk about when we got into this but could you share an example of how commitments like Xander agreeing to do the dishes became a tool for integrity and growth in your household? >> Yeah. So, one of the key pieces that we talk about in coaching is integrity. So, by not like moral and ethical integrity, but doing what you say you're going to do when you say you're going to do it, which I know is a huge frustration with a lot of parents or let's just even be honest, like spouses, like, do you do what you're going to say you're going to do when you said you were going to do it? And um I think one of the things we talk about is the distinction between expectations and agreements. So I I might come with these expectations and another another thing that we talk about is everyone is behaving according to how the world occurs to them. So all of my behavior is flowing from how things are occurring to me in my mind. And this has been so helpful for me as a parent because it's like it's occurring to me one way, it's occurring to them another way. Like it doesn't occur to a 2-year-old to keep a house clean. They're like the messier the better. Like those are signs of life, you know. >> Yes. You're right. But right. But it's occurring to us a whole other way. And so that even just that thinking has helped me. So it's a conversation not necessarily about like the real is about occurrence. Like how is it occurring to me and how is it occurring to them and how can we align on occurrence? So Xander with the the dishes like the integrity is when we I don't have an expectation that the dishes are clean. Like we actually co-create an agreement together and it's not hey Xander I need for you to do the dishes because I'm not going to die if he doesn't. Um but it is hey Xander here's my request. My request is as you're a contributor in this house um my internet is called the Queendom. So they are clear this is what is like here and my request is that you that you would wash the dishes every night before um before you go you'll go go to bed and he says yes. So then we get really clear on what that commitment is. We get clear on what the commitment is for what clean dish actually looks like. we get a commitment around even like what time it is because I think sometimes it was like this time but then um I was like no I actually like the commitment to be this time um but then it isn't like this frustration of you didn't do what you said you were going to do. I mean there is love to be real there is frustration but it became a conversation around a broken commitment like oh okay we just have a broken commitment here. um what was going on with that and it was all the the larger vision was in service of him being a man of his word and it was like right like dander do you want to be a person of your word and it's like well yeah like how do you say no to that you know and so like this is one of the places to like clean dishes sure but it's this is one of the places for you to work out being a person of your word and keeping your commitment to yourself and to me someone who you love. So, it was more every time there was a broken commitment, it was a conversation about all of that, not just, dude, you didn't wash the dishes. Um, nor like a punishment in some way, but it was like, how do we restore trust and how do you want to make that right? So, yeah, it's just a different conversation and it's such a good tool. You don't really think of chores as a tool for teaching integrity and teaching commitment and teaching kind of these interpersonal skills. You know, I I've never really thought about it that way. You know, that's such a benefit to if you're really seeing this young person, this child of yours as a future contributor to his household, as a husband, as you know, a roommate, as a father, whatever. He ends up, you know, being >> and even a co-orker like in the workplace, it works that way. >> Yes. Yes. It's so, you know, what a cool way to teach something without that much at stake, you know. That's right. It is low stakes like really I mean dishes, you know, but um he still like when he's in college and he's thinking about keeping his word, he can remember not necessarily the dishes, but all of the conversation around the importance of being a person of your word and what that that means for you. >> I love that. And so you guys would actually write these out, huh? Would you guys write out your commitments and actually put them in writing? Yes, because otherwise they get lost like so we'll talk about like one of the things in coaching we'll talk like where does your word live and then we would talk about like again in the corporate space like where does your word live so you need to capture it somewhere visible where is the then a system of accountability so we'll talk about like what is your system for maintaining your integrity um so for me I have a sauna you know I have all of these systems in my life and when I don't have those systems I often don't keep my word um to myself um right more often to others less to myself like even conversations with him about that of like okay where is this captured and what is your system to maintaining your integrity is like oh I could set an alarm on my phone for 9:00 p.m. every night that says wash the dishes >> and he comes up with that but like and they know to say you know like not to make excuses like I didn't is like what were you more committed to like I just didn't want to do it like you know but we actually have the conversation around what got in the way of keeping the commitment but yeah capturing it somewhere and creating a system where I'm not the one holding him accountable but what is his ownership of a system to keep his integrity. >> I love it. I really, really do because teaching integrity is like one of those things that it's you don't want to do it with high stakes. It's much easier to do this with the trash and the dishes and, you know, cleaning a toilet or something like that rather than trying to teach integrity with some higher stakes things like maybe even just like cars or whatever we're using to um you know, there's a lot of other examples where you you're going to have to teach them integrity at some point.
